does: This paper systematically investigates how fine-tuning large language models on downstream tasks compromises their safety alignment. The authors evaluate safety degradation across four representative tasks—summarization, code generation, translation, and classification—measuring how many harmful prompts receive harmful responses after fine-tuning. They test multiple model calibrations and safety interventions to quantify the extent of the problem. The key findings show that code generation and translation tasks cause the most severe safety deterioration, translation and classification tasks have inherently weaker safety guardrails with 73-92% of harmful prompts receiving harmful answers, and existing safety solutions like guard mechanisms and safety-tuned datasets fail to maintain robustness across different tasks, revealing critical limitations in current mitigation approaches.
connects: This work extends the emerging literature on safety alignment in large language models, building on prior red-teaming and adversarial robustness research that demonstrated fine-tuning on benign data could degrade safety properties. It responds to the gap between general safety alignment research and task-specific vulnerabilities, combining insights from multitask learning literature with safety concerns. The paper builds on earlier findings about model calibration effects on safety while providing the first systematic cross-task analysis of safety degradation patterns. It connects to work on instruction tuning and RLHF-based alignment by examining whether these methods maintain safety guarantees under realistic fine-tuning scenarios across diverse downstream applications.
enables: This work enables development of more robust safety solutions that generalize across multiple downstream tasks and use cases. By identifying task-specific vulnerability patterns, it points toward adaptive safety strategies that can be tailored to particular fine-tuning objectives. The findings motivate research into multitask-aware safety alignment methods and calibration techniques that preserve safety properties regardless of downstream application. The systematic characterization of which tasks pose the greatest safety risks enables practitioners to make informed choices about deployment and safety testing. This research also opens directions for developing fundamentally more robust fine-tuning approaches, cross-task safety evaluation benchmarks, and unified defense mechanisms that maintain guardrails across diverse model applications in real-world deployment settings.
